geological.name: Hadean Eon
geological.description: The earliest eon of Earth's history, spanning from planetary formation to the first stable continental crust. Named after Hades, the Greek underworld, reflecting its hellish conditions — no intact rock record survives from most of this period.
keyEvents: Formation of Earth by accretion of planetesimals (~4600 Ma)
Giant impact hypothesis: Theia collision forms the Moon (~4500 Ma)
Formation of first oceans from steam condensation (~4400 Ma)
Oldest known mineral: Jack Hills zircon, Australia (~4404 Ma)
Late Heavy Bombardment: intense meteorite flux (~4100–3800 Ma)

geological.name: Archean Eon
geological.description: The Archean saw the formation of the first stable continental cratons and the emergence of life. The atmosphere was largely anoxic, rich in methane and carbon dioxide. Stromatolites — layered structures built by microbial mats — provide the oldest undisputed evidence of life.
keyEvents: Oldest known rocks: Acasta Gneiss, Canada (~3960 Ma)
Earliest chemical evidence of life in Isua rocks (~3700 Ma)
First confirmed stromatolites: Pilbara, Australia (~3480 Ma)
Formation of major cratons: Kaapvaal, Pilbara, Superior
Anoxic oceans and atmosphere throughout entire eon

children.name: Eoarchean Era
children.description: The earliest recognised era of Earth. Characterised by intense meteorite bombardment, magma ocean solidification, and the first emergence of oceanic and proto-continental crust.
keyEvents: Oldest known rocks: Acasta Gneiss, Canada (~3960 Ma)
Nuvvuagittuq Belt — possible microfossils (disputed, ~3770 Ma)
Continued Late Heavy Bombardment into early Eoarchean
First stable oceanic crust appears

children.name: Paleoarchean Era
children.description: Oldest confirmed microbial life. Stromatolites from the Pilbara Craton (Australia) and Kaapvaal Craton (South Africa) provide the most compelling early evidence of biotic activity.
keyEvents: Oldest confirmed microfossils (~3480 Ma): Apex Chert, Pilbara
First stromatolite reefs in shallow marine environments
Widespread hydrothermal vent ecosystems
Formation of Kaapvaal and Pilbara cratons

children.name: Mesoarchean Era
children.description: Growth of stable continental crust accelerated. More extensive stromatolite reefs developed. The first supercontinents may have begun forming through accretion of cratons.
keyEvents: Formation of proto-supercontinent Vaalbara (~3100 Ma)
Expansion of continental crust via accretion
Widespread microbial mats in tidal and shallow-sea environments
Onset of banded iron formation deposition

children.name: Neoarchean Era
children.description: The final Archean era saw stabilisation of the first true continents and possibly the earliest forms of plate tectonics. Cyanobacteria were evolving and beginning to produce oxygen.
keyEvents: Kenorland supercontinent assembly
Early cyanobacterial oxygen production begins
Transition to modern-style plate tectonics (debated)
Widespread greenstone belt formation

geological.name: Proterozoic Eon
geological.description: The Proterozoic ('earlier life') is the longest eon, spanning nearly 2 billion years. It witnessed the Great Oxidation Event, the first eukaryotes, 'Snowball Earth' glaciations, and the assembly of the first widely recognised supercontinents.
keyEvents: Great Oxidation Event: atmospheric O₂ rises dramatically (~2400 Ma)
First eukaryotes with cell nuclei (~2100 Ma)
Rodinia supercontinent assembles (~1100 Ma)
Sturtian Snowball Earth glaciation (~720 Ma)
Marinoan Snowball Earth glaciation (~635 Ma)
Ediacaran biota: first large multicellular organisms (~575 Ma)

children.name: Paleoproterozoic Era
children.description: The Great Oxidation Event dramatically changed Earth's atmosphere and oceans, causing the 'Oxygen Catastrophe' — a mass extinction of anaerobic organisms. The first eukaryotes with cell nuclei appeared.
keyEvents: Great Oxidation Event (~2400 Ma): O₂ rises from <0.001% to ~1%
Huronian glaciation (~2400–2100 Ma)
First eukaryotic cells with nuclei (~2100 Ma)
Banded iron formations reach peak deposition
First red-bed sediments (evidence of oxidising atmosphere)

children.name: Mesoproterozoic Era
children.description: A relatively stable period with low evolutionary innovation, sometimes called the 'Boring Billion'. The supercontinent Rodinia assembled, and the first sexually reproducing eukaryotes appeared.
keyEvents: Assembly of supercontinent Rodinia (~1100 Ma)
First sexual reproduction in eukaryotes
Earliest red and green algae
Stable climate and marine chemistry for ~1 billion years

children.name: Neoproterozoic Era
children.description: Dramatic climate events (Snowball Earth), breakup of Rodinia, and the explosion of complex multicellular life (Ediacaran biota) marked this era — setting the stage for the Cambrian Explosion.
keyEvents: Breakup of Rodinia supercontinent (~750 Ma)
Sturtian Snowball Earth: global glaciation (~720 Ma)
Marinoan Snowball Earth: second global glaciation (~635 Ma)
Ediacaran biota: first large multicellular organisms (~575 Ma)
First animals with hard shells appear (~550 Ma)

geological.name: Phanerozoic Eon
geological.description: The 'visible life' eon. Spanning just 541 million years but containing all familiar animal life — from trilobites to dinosaurs to humans. Defined by the Cambrian Explosion and punctuated by five major mass extinctions.
keyEvents: Cambrian Explosion: rapid diversification of animal body plans (~538 Ma)
First land plants (~470 Ma)
First terrestrial vertebrates: Tiktaalik (~375 Ma)
End-Permian extinction: ~90–96% of species lost (~252 Ma)
Dinosaurs dominate for 165 million years
Chicxulub impact: end of non-avian dinosaurs (~66 Ma)
First Homo sapiens (~0.3 Ma)

children.name: Paleozoic Era
children.description: The 'ancient life' era saw the explosion of marine animal diversity, colonisation of land by plants and animals, and formation of vast coal forests. Ended with the End-Permian extinction, the largest in Earth's history.
keyEvents: Cambrian Explosion (~538 Ma)
First land plants: bryophyte-like (~470 Ma)
First jawed vertebrates (~430 Ma)
First amphibians: Tiktaalik (~375 Ma)
Assembly of Pangaea (~300 Ma)
End-Permian extinction (~252 Ma): ~90% of species lost

children.name: Cambrian Period
children.description: The Cambrian Explosion produced most modern animal body plans in a geologically rapid radiation over ~20 million years. Trilobites dominated the seas. The Burgess Shale provides an extraordinary window into this world.
keyEvents: Rapid diversification of all major animal phyla (~538 Ma)
Burgess Shale exceptional fossil fauna (~508 Ma)
Trilobites diversify into hundreds of species
Archaeocyatha (first reef builders) appear and go extinct
First predators with eyes: Anomalocaris

children.name: Ordovician Period
children.description: A great diversification of marine life (Great Ordovician Biodiversification Event). Ended with the second largest mass extinction in Earth's history, triggered by Gondwana glaciation.
keyEvents: Great Ordovician Biodiversification Event
First vertebrates: jawless fish (Agnatha)
First coral reefs appear
Gondwana drifts over the South Pole → glaciation
End-Ordovician extinction (~445 Ma): ~85% of species lost

children.name: Silurian Period
children.description: Life recovered and expanded after the Ordovician extinction. First vascular plants colonised the land and the first jawed vertebrates appeared in the seas.
keyEvents: First vascular plants on land: Cooksonia
First jawed vertebrates (gnathostomes)
Coral reefs expanded globally
Stable warm climate throughout most of the period
First terrestrial arthropods (scorpions, millipedes)

children.name: Devonian Period
children.description: The 'Age of Fishes'. First forests transformed the atmosphere; first amphibians emerged from the sea. Ended with the Devonian mass extinction.
keyEvents: First forests: Archaeopteris trees (~380 Ma)
First amphibians: Tiktaalik (~375 Ma)
First seed plants
Sharks diversify; armoured fish (placoderms) dominate early
Late Devonian extinction (~372 Ma): ~75% of species lost

children.name: Carboniferous Period
children.description: Vast coal swamp forests dominated equatorial regions, producing the coal deposits burned today. First reptiles, giant insects, and winged insects evolved in oxygen-rich air.
keyEvents: Vast coal swamp forests (Lepidodendron, Sigillaria)
First amniotic eggs → first true reptiles
Giant arthropods: Arthropleura (2m millipede), Meganeura (70cm dragonfly)
First winged insects
Carboniferous Rainforest Collapse (~307 Ma)

children.name: Permian Period
children.description: The Permian saw the complete assembly of Pangaea and the rise of synapsids — the group that eventually gave rise to mammals. Ended with the most devastating extinction in Earth's history.
keyEvents: Assembly of Pangaea supercontinent
Rise of synapsids (Dimetrodon, Dicynodon)
Permian interior deserts and arid red-bed deposits
Siberian Traps massive volcanism begins (~252 Ma)
End-Permian extinction (~252 Ma): ~90–96% of species lost

children.name: Mesozoic Era
children.description: The 'Age of Reptiles' — dinosaurs, marine reptiles, and pterosaurs dominated. First flowering plants, first birds, and first mammals appeared. Ended with the Chicxulub asteroid impact.
keyEvents: Recovery from End-Permian extinction
Dinosaurs diversify and dominate (~230 Ma)
First mammals (~225 Ma)
Breakup of Pangaea into Laurasia and Gondwana
First flowering plants (angiosperms, ~130 Ma)
First birds: Archaeopteryx (~150 Ma)
Chicxulub impact (~66 Ma): 75% of species lost

children.name: Triassic Period
children.description: Life rebuilt after the End-Permian extinction. Dinosaurs, crocodilians, pterosaurs, and mammals all arose during the Triassic. Pangaea began to rift.
keyEvents: Recovery from largest mass extinction on record
First dinosaurs (~230 Ma): Eoraptor, Herrerasaurus
First mammals (~225 Ma): small, nocturnal
First pterosaurs (~228 Ma)
Onset of Pangaea rifting
Triassic–Jurassic extinction (~201 Ma)

children.name: Jurassic Period
children.description: Peak dinosaur diversity. Pangaea broke apart, warm humid conditions prevailed globally, and the first birds evolved from theropod dinosaurs.
keyEvents: Pangaea breaks into Laurasia and Gondwana
Sauropods reach maximum sizes: Brachiosaurus, Diplodocus
Archaeopteryx: evolutionary link between dinosaurs and birds (~150 Ma)
Marine reptiles diversify: Ichthyosaurs, Plesiosaurs
Warm, high sea levels; no polar ice caps

children.name: Cretaceous Period
children.description: The longest Mesozoic period. Flowering plants diversified explosively, fundamentally transforming terrestrial ecosystems. Ended with the Chicxulub asteroid impact.
keyEvents: Explosive diversification of flowering plants (angiosperms)
India separates from Gondwana and begins northward drift
Tyrannosaurus rex (~68 Ma)
Triceratops, Velociraptor, Pachycephalosaurus
Chicxulub asteroid impact (~66 Ma): 10–15 km body strikes Yucatán
75–80% of species lost including all non-avian dinosaurs

children.name: Cenozoic Era
children.description: The 'Age of Mammals'. After the dinosaurs' extinction, mammals diversified explosively. Continents drifted to current positions, Antarctica froze, grasslands spread, and Homo sapiens emerged.
keyEvents: Explosive mammalian radiation after dinosaur extinction
India–Asia collision: Himalayas begin to rise (~50 Ma)
Antarctica glaciation begins (~34 Ma)
Grasslands spread globally (~23 Ma)
Hominids diverge from chimpanzees (~7 Ma)
First Homo sapiens (~0.3 Ma)
Human civilisation (~0.01 Ma)

children.name: Paleogene Period
children.description: Mammals diversified rapidly filling niches left by dinosaurs. Global temperatures were initially much warmer (Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum). First primates, whales, and large herbivores appeared.
keyEvents: Mammals diversify rapidly: >10 new orders within 10 Myr
Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum: +5–8°C globally (~56 Ma)
First primates (~55 Ma)
First whales (Pakicetus, ~50 Ma)
India–Asia collision: Himalayas begin (~50 Ma)
Antarctic glaciation begins (~34 Ma)

children.name: Neogene Period
children.description: Global cooling and grassland expansion drove major faunal changes. Hominids diverged from other apes. The Mediterranean Sea dried up completely (Messinian Salinity Crisis).
keyEvents: Grassland biomes spread across every continent
Great Apes diversify (~15 Ma): Gigantopithecus, Sivapithecus
Messinian Salinity Crisis: Mediterranean dries up (~5.9–5.3 Ma)
Hominids diverge from chimpanzees (~7 Ma)
Australopithecus afarensis — 'Lucy' (~3.9–3.0 Ma)

children.name: Quaternary Period
children.description: Characterised by repeated glacial cycles (ice ages) and the evolution and global spread of Homo sapiens. The present interglacial (Holocene) has seen the rise of all human civilisations.
keyEvents: Pleistocene ice ages begin: 2.6 Ma — glacial cycles every ~100 kyr
Homo erectus leaves Africa (~1.8 Ma)
Denisovans and Neanderthals diverge (~0.7 Ma)
Homo sapiens evolves in Africa (~300,000 yr ago)
Out-of-Africa migration (~70,000–50,000 yr ago)
Last glacial maximum (~20,000 yr ago): ice sheets cover 30% of land
Holocene begins: all human civilisations arise (~11,700 yr ago)

cosmic.name: Big Bang
cosmic.description: The origin of space, time, and all matter-energy. In the first microseconds, quarks and gluons filled a superhot plasma. Within 3 minutes, protons and neutrons fused into the first atomic nuclei (Big Bang Nucleosynthesis).

cosmic.name: Cosmic Microwave Background
cosmic.description: 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe cooled enough (~3000 K) for electrons and protons to combine into neutral hydrogen. The universe became transparent to light, releasing the CMB radiation still detectable today.

cosmic.name: Cosmic Dark Ages
cosmic.description: Between the CMB and first stars (~200–400 Myr), the universe was opaque to visible light. Only neutral hydrogen and helium filled the cosmos, slowly clumping under gravity.

cosmic.name: First Stars (Population III)
cosmic.description: Massive stars (100–1000 M☉) composed almost entirely of hydrogen and helium. They forged the first heavy elements in their cores and seeded the universe with metals when they exploded as supernovae.

cosmic.name: Epoch of Reionization
cosmic.description: Radiation from the first stars and quasars reionised the surrounding hydrogen gas, making the universe transparent again to ultraviolet light. Ends the Cosmic Dark Ages.

cosmic.name: First Galaxies
cosmic.description: The first protogalaxies assembled under gravity from merging dark matter halos and gas clouds. The James Webb Space Telescope has observed galaxies from just 300 Myr after the Big Bang.

cosmic.name: Milky Way Forms
cosmic.description: Our galaxy began assembling from merging protogalactic clouds and satellite galaxies. The oldest stars in the Milky Way date to ~13.2 billion years ago — almost as old as the universe itself.

cosmic.name: Solar System Forms
cosmic.description: A dense region in a molecular cloud collapses under gravity, forming the proto-Sun and a rotating protoplanetary disk of gas and dust. Within a few million years, planets accrete from this disk.

cosmic.name: Earth & Moon Form
cosmic.description: Earth grows by accretion of planetesimals. A Mars-sized body (Theia) collides with proto-Earth in a giant impact, ejecting material that coalesces into the Moon. Earth's iron core segregates.

cosmic.name: First Life on Earth
cosmic.description: Chemical isotope evidence of biological carbon fixation in the Isua Greenstone Belt, Greenland (~3.7 Ga). Microbial life likely thrived near hydrothermal vents in the early anoxic oceans.

cosmic.name: Great Oxidation Event
cosmic.description: Cyanobacteria evolved oxygenic photosynthesis. Atmospheric O₂ rose from <0.001% to ~1–2%, poisoning most anaerobic life ('Oxygen Catastrophe') but enabling aerobic metabolism — the foundation for all complex life.

cosmic.name: First Eukaryotes
cosmic.description: Cells with a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles evolve, likely via endosymbiosis: an archaeal host cell engulfs a proteobacterium that became the mitochondrion.

cosmic.name: Snowball Earth
cosmic.description: Global glaciation events (Sturtian ~720 Ma and Marinoan ~635 Ma) covered Earth in ice from poles to equator. The thaw may have triggered an explosion of complex life.

cosmic.name: First Complex Multicellular Life
cosmic.description: Ediacaran biota (~575 Ma): the first large, complex, multicellular organisms appear — soft-bodied, enigmatic creatures with no modern analogues, possibly representing early animal phyla.

cosmic.name: Cambrian Explosion
cosmic.description: Rapid diversification of most animal body plans in the fossil record over ~20 million years. Eyes, shells, limbs, and active predation all appear suddenly. The 'Big Bang of biology'.

cosmic.name: First Land Plants
cosmic.description: Bryophyte-like plants colonise land, fundamentally altering the carbon cycle, soil formation, and the chemistry of rivers. Paved the way for terrestrial animal life.

cosmic.name: End-Permian Extinction
cosmic.description: The most severe mass extinction in Earth's history wiped out ~90–96% of all species. Likely caused by the Siberian Traps super-volcanism triggering runaway climate change.

cosmic.name: Chicxulub Impact
cosmic.description: A 10–15 km asteroid struck the Yucatán Peninsula at ~20 km/s, releasing energy equivalent to ~1 billion Hiroshima bombs. The resulting impact winter ended 75% of species, including all non-avian dinosaurs.

cosmic.name: Homo sapiens
cosmic.description: Anatomically modern humans evolve in Africa, likely in multiple populations. Brain volume reaches ~1400 cm³. Symbolic art, language, and long-distance trade appear by ~100,000 yr ago.

cosmic.name: Human Civilisation
cosmic.description: The Neolithic revolution (~10,000 BCE): agriculture, permanent settlements, and the first cities. Writing, metallurgy, and organised states follow — compressing geological timescales into human history.

cosmic.name: Today
cosmic.description: You are here. Earth has been a living world for 3.8 billion years. The universe is 46× older than Earth. Homo sapiens have existed for 0.007% of Earth's history.
